video_title: Ecological interactions | Middle school Biology | Khan Academy

segments.text: This is the African savannah. Here you
see grazing zebras, willbees moving in
herds, elephants pulling up grass,
giraffes, and lions, and so many other
animals all at the same place and time.
But how can so many different organisms
who all need food, water, and space, how
can they all live together in the same
ecosystem? I mean, wouldn't they
constantly like, I don't know, maybe get
in each other's way or something? Well,
to understand this, scientists study
something called the ecological
interactions. What is that? Let's find
out. So, this ecological interactions,
this describes how organisms affect one
another as they live in the same
environment. What I mean is it explains
how an ecosystem functions as a whole.
It tells us about how its population um
survives or grows or you know even
shrinks over time.
And there are three major types of
ecological interactions. We have
competition, predation and herbivory.
And we have symbiosis which itself has a
few other subtypes.
So let's start with competition.
You probably know that all organisms
need resources to survive. They need
food for energy. They need water. They
need space to live. They need safe
places to rest and reproduce and so on.
But in any ecosystem, these resources
are limited. I mean, there's only so
much grass or there's only so much
water, right? So, competition happens
when organisms need the same limited
resource. For example, on the savannah,
the zebras and the wilderbeast both feed
on grasses. Now, if many animals are
grazing in the same area, there may not
be enough grass for all of them. That is
competition. Notice that competition
does not necessarily mean fighting. I
mean, these zebras and wilderbees don't
have to fight each other to compete. If
one group ends up eating more grass,
then less grass remains for the other
group, right? That alone is competition.
And by the way, competition can happen
between different species like zebras
and the werbees. Or it can happen
between the same species like between
two zebras or two wheelbees feeding in
the same area. And what do you think is
going to happen if this competition
becomes very intense?
Well, if resources become too limited,
some organisms may not even get enough
food or water or space and that can
reduce reproduction and lower population
sizes all together. But here's something
interesting. Sometimes organisms can
reduce competition. How? By using
resources differently. For example,
giraffes feed high in acasia trees while
zebras or wabbees, for example, mostly
feed on the grass close to the ground.
So, because they're using different
sources of food, they compete less
directly, right? So you can see how
competition can help shape where
organisms live and how many can survive
in an ecosystem.
All right, next up let's talk about
predation herby worry. Let's start with
predation. Some organisms survive by
feeding on other organisms. So when one
organism hunts and kills and eats
another organism, that's what we call
predation. In this example, the lion is
the predator and the antelope over here
is the prey. So, the antelope is killed
and the lion gains the nutrition and
energy. Okay, now think about this. If
the prey population increases in a
certain area, what do you think is going
to happen to the predator population in
that area? Well, now since there's more
food available, the predator population
would also increase, right? In contrast,
what if the prey population were to
reduce in a particular area? Well, now
less food is available. So, there will
be more competition and that would tend
to reduce the predator population in the
area. So, notice how these interactions
are connected, right? So, predation
helps shape the balance of populations
in an ecosystem.
But remember, not all feeding
interactions involve animals eating
other animals. What about animals eating
plants? That's called herbivory.
For example, giraffes eat acasia trees.
Elephants eat grass. These animals are
herbivores. In this particular
interaction, again, the animal gains
energy and nutrients and the plant is
damaged. So again, similar question.
What if we have an area with lots of
plants? What would happen to the herby
population in that area? Well, again
there's more food available, so the
herbivore population in that area would
increase. But again, if too many
herbivores feed in one area, what would
happen to the plants? The plant
population may decrease and that will
now again affect the herbivore
population. Right? So you can see just
like with predation even herbuy
influences survival, reproduction,
population sizes, all of that. This now
brings us to the third major type of
ecological interaction, symbiosis.
What exactly is this? Well, take a look
at this picture. It might look like
these birds are troubling the buffalo,
but they're not. These birds are called
oxpecker birds, and they usually sit on
large mammals like buffaloos. But why?
Because they get to eat the ticks and
other parasites from the mammals's skin.
Why would they do that? because the
birds gain their food this way and the
mammals end up having their parasites
removed. So look, both organisms are
benefiting. That is an example of
symbiosis. It's a type of ecological
interaction in which organisms form
close long-term relationships with each
other. Now this particular kind of
symbiosis in which both the organisms
are benefiting, we call it mutualism.
And notice the birds are not trying to
sort of like help the buffalo. No, no,
no. They're just trying to eat the food
and the buffalo ends up benefiting as a
natural result of that behavior, right?
But this is not the only kind of
symbiosis. Take a look at this video.
What do you notice? Hey, you see these
tiny birds following the elephant,
right?
These birds are called cattle eigrits
and they usually follow large grazing
animals like elephants or zebras. But
why do they do that? Well, because as
these large animals move through grass,
insects are stirred up. So the birds
catch and eat those insects. So who
benefits this time? The birds do. But
what about the elephants? They are not
benefited at all but they are also not
harmed. Right? This is called
commensalism. It's also a type of
symbiosis because again there is a
closed long-term relationship between
them but it's a kind in which one
organism benefits but the other is
mostly unaffected.
And so now you can see where we're going
with this. So you can kind of predict
what the third one is going to be. For
that, take an example of a tick feeding
on some mammals's blood, like deer's
blood for example. What's happening over
here? Well, notice the tick gains
nutrients, but what happens to the
mammal? It loses blood. It might become
irritated or weakened. So, this time,
notice one organism benefits, but the
other is harmed.
This type of symbiosis is called
parasitism. And so the tick becomes a
parasite. And again, what's important is
that this is not intentional harm. The
tick is not trying to harm the mammal
over here. It's simply a survival
strategy.
So coming back to our original question,
how can so many organisms live together
in an ecosystem? The answer lies in
ecological interactions.
All these organisms will compete for
limited resources. They will feed on one
another and they will also form
long-term relationships.
All these interactions will influence
who survives, who reproduces, and how
many organisms can live in a particular
area. So, what's incredible is that
ecosystems are not random collections of
organisms. Hopefully, we can now
appreciate how they are continuously
shaped by various kinds of interactions.
